paragraphs.source_preview: Prices are doubling again, Supercycle is bigger, and could last longer than you think
By Dylan Patel, Ray Wang, Myron Xie, and 2 others
Feb 06, 2026 · Paid

sections.title: The Inevitability of Memory Cycles: A History of Booms and Busts

paragraphs.source_preview: Since its commercial introduction in the 1970s, DRAM has benefited from the two scaling laws that defined the semiconductor industry: Moore’s Law and Dennard sc
For much of the industry’s history, DRAM density scaled faster than logic, doubling roughly every 18 months instead of 24 months and driving dramatic cost reduc
For more details on the industry and DRAM basics, check out our technical deep dive:
The Memory Wall: Past, Present, and Future of DRAM - Dylan Patel, Jeff Koch, and 3 others · September 3, 2024
Yet DRAM scaling has slowed significantly over the past few decades, and density gains over time have shrunk. Over the past decade, DRAM density has increased b
Bitlines and sense amplifiers, once secondary concerns, are now dominant constraints. Every incremental shrink reduces signal margin, increases variability, and
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CvGg!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: Micron
An easy way to understand the technical challenge in DRAM scaling is to think of a DRAM cell as a tiny bucket that holds electricity instead of water. Each buck
This is a problem. When the system tries to read the data, it has to detect this very faint electrical signal and distinguish it from noise. The wires that conn
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Lw1D!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
Together, these constraints explain why DRAM density has stagnated and why DRAM scaling has slowed down significantly over the years. The collapse of DRAM scali
As density gains slow, cost per bit reductions have slowed down. DRAM pricing is now more dependent on capacity additions and cyclical supply-demand dynamics ra

sections.title: Memory Cycle Part II: Key Features of a Cycle

paragraphs.source_preview: The memory industry has been defined by commoditization, which comes with cyclicality. This outcome reflects a combination of industry-wide competitive behavior
At its core, memory’s cyclicality is driven by timing mismatches between demand changes and corresponding supply responses. Aside from the buffer of short-term
Memory manufacturing, much like logic, is among the most capital-intensive industries in the world. Building leading-edge DRAM and NAND fabs requires multi-bill
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7rCY!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
These heavy capital investments mean that suppliers need to operate with high utilization rates to generate cash profits to earn a payback. However, utilization
Still, because the majority of the cost is already sunk, the fab is built and equipment purchased, suppliers are better off running wafers so long as they can s
Memory supply can be expanded by migrating to more advanced process nodes with yield improvements, which increases bit supply without requiring new “greenfield”
The impact of node migration, however, is dynamic over time. When a new node is first introduced, initial yields are typically lower, limiting effective output
Another thing is that node transitions do not halt simply because demand weakens. Consequently, bit supply growth can remain robust well into downturns. This ex
In a downturn, the impact of pricing declines can be existential for memory suppliers. By the time pricing rolls over, manufacturers have already committed and
The risks inherent in “memory economics” are high. A highly commoditized product with elastic demand versus a capex-heavy, long-timeline, inelastic supply produ
During the Windows PC supercycle of the early to mid-1990s, there were approximately 20 meaningful DRAM suppliers. Elevated demand and strong pricing attracted
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hXg_!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
From a demand perspective, memory consumption is not always linear or predictable. During mature phases of existing product cycles, demand growth can be relativ
There have been several of these product cycle inflections over the past few decades. New computing platforms like the PC, the smartphone, cloud computing, and
However, such inflection-driven upcycles have not been sustainable in the long run. Prior memory supercycles have tended to peak and roll into downcycles within
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TIHQ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
From a financial market perspective, participants are forward-looking. Investors anticipate shifts in the supply–demand balance and pricing well before supplier
There are a few especially interesting examples, worth revisiting in the context of the current memory supercycle: the 1993 Windows PC supercycle, the cloud and
During the 1993 memory supercycle, the DRAM industry entered a strong upcycle driven by the rapid adoption of Windows PCs and the broad implementation of graphi
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!KY1M!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
On the supply side, the industry was emerging from a prolonged downturn in the late 1980s because of intensified competition, declining margins, and a significa
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!H3iI!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis estimates
Unsurprisingly, this set the stage for a downturn. Japanese incumbents and then-emerging Korean players responded with aggressive capacity expansion—bringing ne
Rapid yield improvements further amplified supply, driving a sharp increase in bits per wafer. By 1995–1996, the market gradually flipped from shortage to overs
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qAAO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: Integrated Circuit Engineering, SemiAnalysis
The 2010 memory supercycle started in roughly the 2nd half of 2009, driven by two simultaneous demand inflections: (1) the smartphone rapid adoption globally, l
On top of this, smartphone demand growth was a near-vertical volume step-up. Global smartphonec by broader Android price-tier penetration and iPhone scaling. On
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5i8t!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis estimates
Similar to prior cycles, the sudden demand surge combined with constrained supply drove a meaningful increase in DRAM pricing. What differentiated the 2010 cycl
Moreover, DRAM pricing in this cycle peaked earlier and rolled over more quickly than in previous upcycles. Pricing momentum proved difficult to sustain even wi
Another key development of this cycle was the rapid standardization of mobile DRAM which further accelerated commoditization. LPDDR2 was standardized by JEDEC i
The 2017–2018 memory supercycle is still a fresh memory for many in the industry and is oft-cited as a good analogue for today’s supercycle. On the demand side,
This pricing environment translated directly into record financial performance, peaking in 2H18. Memory suppliers generated unprecedented levels of free cash fl
Yet, as with the prior cycles, the fundamental cyclical dynamics of the industry prevailed. A few familiar factors developed in late 2018 and 2019: (1) supply g
The most recent DRAM upcycle was catalyzed during the COVID-era “chip crisis,” an unprecedented synchronization of demand shock and supply disruption. Global lo
Beyond surging end-demand, purchasing behavior amplified the cycle, especially from the enterprise side. OEMs, hyperscalers, and channel partners were taking a
From the DRAM suppliers’ perspective, it became increasingly difficult to distinguish true end-demand from panic ordering. The result was a rapid drawdown of in
On the supply side, the industry was structurally constrained. During the pandemic, suppliers faced additional friction from labor shortages, logistics disrupti
Capex decisions during this period were therefore cautious and selective. Rather than aggressive greenfield expansion, memory suppliers prioritized node migrati
Crucially, this cycle reset industry behavior and expectations. Memory suppliers emerged with a stronger appreciation for disciplined capex, tighter inventory m

sections.title: AI-Driven Memory Supercycle: Bigger, Longer, and a Shortage that is Harder to Solve

paragraphs.source_preview: For those who have lived through multiple memory cycles, the central question when it comes to this supercycle is the same: when will this cycle peak? It is nat
Currently, the DRAM industry is operating in a deeply supply-constrained environment, and based on our Memory Industry Model, we believe the supply–demand imbal
Below, we share:
Our forecast for DRAM and HBM supply/demand mismatch from our Memory Industry Model, through 2027
HBM4 qualification: how is each supplier doing, market share % for Rubin
DRAM wafer- and bit-capacity data with HBM broken out
Upgrades forecast: trends in wafer capacity by node with fab-by-fab details
DRAM pricing forecast out through 2027
Last, we discuss the factors and timing for the end of the cycle
As a bonus, we’ve also included DRAM EUV layer trends and WFE capex forecasts
…7% below demand in 2026. Within this, the HBM shortfall is expected to widen from ~5% this year to ~6% in 2026, and further to ~9% in 2027.
Importantly, commodity DRAM is also forecast to remain structurally tight, with an estimated ~7% supply deficit persisting through both 2026 and 2027. At this d
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!6F4h!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
This magnitude of shortage is rare in the modern history of the memory industry, both in terms of its severity and its duration. Even during the 2017–2018 memor
In our view, three key and interrelated drivers underpin this ongoing supercycle and are likely to sustain the supply shortfall through 2027 and potentially 202
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lsKv!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
First is the accelerating HBM/DRAM demand and HBM’s rapidly expanding capacity. The surge in compute demand is translating into a sharp increase in memory requi
Beginning around 2023, we have observed a clear and steadily accelerating trend in HBM demand, accompanied by increasingly aggressive responses from memory supp
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-f7p!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
And the capacity expansion in HBM is far from complete. We believe additional capacity expansion is already underway, with HBM wafer capacity reaching approxima
But what’s important here is not just the significant capacity expansion in HBM, but also a meaningful trade-off between HBM and commodity DRAM wafer allocation
The impact of materially expanding HBM capacity is unambiguous. When we examine HBM’s wafer intensity within overall DRAM production, we find that HBM wafer cap
One may ask why memory makers are moving so aggressively to expand HBM capacity. We believe the rationale is twofold. First, HBM is increasingly viewed as a str
Second, HBM’s complex front-end and back-end manufacturing requirements enable greater product differentiation relative to commodity DRAM. Performance character
Yet, such a significant expansion of HBM capacity carries meaningful cost and strategic challenges. As memory manufacturers increasingly allocate both existing
The first-order impact requires little explanation: reallocating wafer capacity to HBM directly reduces the number of wafers available for commodity DRAM produc
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WeYd!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
HBM uses larger die sizes as each HBM stack consists of multiple large DRAM dies optimized for bandwidth rather than density. HBM’s larger die size is due to th
HBM also requires TSV and a series of incremental process steps—including wafer thinning and backside processing—that do not exist in standard DRAM manufacturin
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rRPm!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: Applied Materials
Stacking and advanced packaging (primary bonding) yields also meaningfully impact HBM output. Even when individual DRAM die yields are high, the compounded yiel
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8I-r!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SK Hynix
We think what distinguishes this cycle is that the product transition driving demand - HBM - is far more manufacturing-intensive, effectively introducing a form
NVIDIA, for example, the transition from Blackwell to Blackwell Ultra and Rubin increases HBM capacity by ~50%, with Rubin Ultra driving a further ~4× jump in s
We estimate that HBM demand alone will account for more than 10% of total DRAM demand by the end of 2027, layered on top of the already substantial server DRAM
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Deks!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
The HBM–DRAM conundrum becomes even more complex when factoring in market reactions to the worsening DRAM shortage driven by HBM capacity reallocation. As commo
We view this as a significant and crucial challenge for memory suppliers. Given the combination of an attractive margin outlook and superior bit output from man
One major contributor to the current shortage that we have not yet discussed in depth is limited cleanroom availability. Following the memory upcycle during the
As we track DRAM capacity additions across all major suppliers, we estimate that virtually all incremental wafer capacity in 2026 will be concentrated in just t
Samsung’s P4 Phase 4 is likely to come online later this year, following the fully equipped Phase 1 and 3. Although Phase 2 of P4 was initially scheduled to com
SK hynix’s Yongin Phase 1 is not expected to bring its cleanroom online until February 2027, with only limited wafer output beginning in 3Q27 due to time for eq
Even Micron’s recently acquired P5 Tongluo fab from PSMC is unlikely to contribute meaningful wafer output until 2H27. At full utilization, the site offers a ma
With these constraints in mind, the central question is how the industry can meaningfully expand bit supply. We currently forecast ~21% bit growth this year and
Accordingly, we expect the combined 1b and 1c capacity across the three major memory suppliers to increase by approximately 80% from year-end 4Q25 through 4Q27.

Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
This is a storyline we have mentioned and seen in previous DRAM upcycles, where the memory suppliers are leveraging node migration for more bit output to fulfil
Yet one should note that DRAM node migration introduces meaningful execution friction. New tool sets must be installed, process windows re-optimized, and yields

sections.title: Supercycle Could be a Double-Edged Sword

paragraphs.source_preview: Unsurprisingly, the first dynamic to emerge in a memory supercycle is an accelerating price uptrend across DRAM products, consistent with patterns observed in p
As we believe the structural shortage is unlikely to improve and the supply environment is set to worsen, DRAM pricing could increase by another ~2× on a 4Q25 v
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!M-HO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Memory Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
Memory price increases are clearly beneficial for memory manufacturers, they come at the expense of customers and the broader end markets. Rising memory content
Apple’s latest earnings commentary confirmed this and suggested that the larger impact from memory will begin in 2H26, despite more favorable positioning in 1H2
In contrast, Tier-1.5 and Tier-2 mobile OEMs—particularly Chinese vendors—face significantly greater margin stress given limited ability to pass through higher
A similar dynamic is emerging in PCs, where OEMs have already implemented selective price increases and are considering specification downgrades in mid- and low
As a result, we remain cautious—and in some cases bearish—on select names across this ecosystem. Given the potential impact on demand elasticity, it will be cri
Beyond mobile, PCs, and other consumer electronics, we believe it is also important to consider the impact of “memoryflation” on AI servers and ODMs as the indu
For further details, please refer to our AI TCO Model and Core Research, where we provide a detailed BoM breakdown and analysis of memory’s impact on AI server
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h9Ft!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: SemiAnalysis Core Research, AI Cloud TCO Model, and Memory Model
Unlike commodity DRAM, HBM typically provides suppliers with greater visibility and long-term volume commitments, but it also limits the ability to raise prices
The pricing impact is evident, but in reality, it has proven far more significant than many expected. The rapid escalation in commodity DRAM pricing is now infl
Back in early 4Q25, market consensus shifted toward a roughly 15–20% ASP decline for HBM3E 12-hi in 2026. This will not be the case in our view, which we noted
It’s also important to address the HBM4 qualification progress: based on our supply-chain checks, the latest HBM4 engineering samples from memory suppliers rema
At a more granular level, Samsung’s HBM4 front-end (1c) appears competitive on both performance and power efficiency, in some cases consuming less power than SK
That said, even as Samsung narrows the performance gap in HBM4, SK Hynix’s proven track record and reliability continue to position it as the dominant HBM suppl
As we move into 2Q this year, we expect to gain improved visibility into the HBM4 competitive landscape. Memory suppliers should continue to enhance HBM4 qualit
We believe the technical complexity of HBM4 could pressure supplier margins due to a more difficult yield learning curve and slow the pace of the product mix tr
History suggests that even the largest memory supercycles do not last indefinitely, and we believe this cycle will be no exception. That said, as we have previo
More fab pull-ins and capacity expansion: We expect additional fab schedule pull-ins across all three major memory suppliers as capacity expansion plans acceler
Significant node migration: As previously outlined, we believe the industry will undergo large-scale node migration to 1b and 1c over the next two years, which
Ongoing HBM acceleration and sustained pressure on commodity: We believe memory suppliers will continue to prioritize HBM as their top strategic focus, given it
A unifying theme across these three developments - fab pull-ins, accelerated node migration, and increased HBM prioritization - is rising demand for WFE. To ena
We expect this dynamic to increasingly extend into the memory ecosystem, benefiting both front-end and back-end equipment suppliers. In fact, just this week, AS
All 3 leading memory producers are increasing EUV layer counts in newer processes. This is necessary for scaling in general, and helps with HBM performance as i

Source: SemiAnalysis WFE Model - Sales@SemiAnalysis.com
